coredata.dc:title: Adsorption and anticorrosion mechanism of glucose-based functionalized carbon dots for copper in neutral solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wan, Shan
Chen, Huikai
Liao, Bokai
Guo, Xingpeng
coredata.dc:description:
Background This work aims at developing some environmental-friendly corrosion inhibitors for copper in neutral solution.
Methods A glucose-based functionalized carbon dots (FCDs) was synthesized through a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. The inhibition performance of FCDs was firstly researched systematically on copper in neutral 3.5 wt% NaCl solution by potentiodynamic polarization curves, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and scanning vibrating electrode technique. The above electrochemical results show that FCDs is an effective corrosion inhibitor for copper in neutral solution, and its optimal corrosion inhibition efficiency exceeds to 88% at 70 ppm. The protective performance of FCDs depends on the coverage of adsorption film on copper surface. Its adsorption isotherm is in accordance with Langmuir adsorption model, involving both physisorption and chemisorption. Atomic force-distance curve directly quantifies the adhesion force between the adsorption film and copper surface.
Significant finds The adsorption and anticorrosion mechanism of FCDs in neutral solution is proposed, which could provide theoretical guidance for design and synthesis of nanomaterial corrosion inhibitor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Copper
Neutral solution
Adsorption
Corrosion inhibition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The characterizations of FCDs and corrosion products

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR, XRD, XPS spectra and morphology of FCDs inhibitor: (a) FTIR, (b) XRD, (c) XPS-full spectrum, (d) XPS-C 1 s, (e) XPS-S 2p, (f) XPS-N 1 s, (g) TEM images and (h) its enlarged view.

EIS plots of Cu in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with different concentrations of FCDs inhibitor: 0 ppm (a1∼a3), 30 ppm (b1∼b3), 50 ppm (c1∼c3), 70 ppm (d1∼d3) and 100 ppm (e1∼e3); corresponding equivalent circuit of EIS plots without (a) and with (b) FCDs.
Potential difference distribution of Cu after different immersion time in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution without and with 70 ppm FCDs inhibitor: 1 h (a1, a2), 6 h (b1, b2), 12 h (c1, c2) and 24 h (d1, d2).
Polarization curves of Cu after 24 h immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with different concentrations of FCDs inhibitor.

Typical force-distance curves (a), corresponding forces (b), SEM micromorphology measured on the Cu samples after 24 h immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution in the absence (c) and presence (d) of 70 ppm FCDs inhibitor.
Inhibition efficiency (a) and adsorption isotherms of Cu surface in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution containing FCDs inhibitor under different immersion time: 4 h (b), 6 h (c), and 8 h (d).
Schematic diagram for adsorption and protection mechanism of FCDs on copper surface in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.
The corresponding EIS fitting results in Fig. 3.
The corresponding PDP fitting results in Fig. 5.

head.ce:title: Adsorption and anticorrosion mechanism of glucose-based functionalized carbon dots for copper in neutral solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Institute of Guangzhou University & Institute of Corrosion Science and Technology, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Institute of Guangzhou University & Institute of Corrosion Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aJoint Institute of Guangzhou University & Institute of Corrosion Science and Technology, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou Key Laboratory for Clean Energy and Materials, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou Key Laboratory for Clean Energy and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou Key Laboratory for Clean Energy and Materials, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou, Guangdong, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work aims at developing some environmental-friendly corrosion inhibitors for copper in neutral solution.

A glucose-based functionalized carbon dots (FCDs) was synthesized through a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. The inhibition performance of FCDs was firstly researched systematically on copper in neutral 3.5 wt% NaCl solution by potentiodynamic polarization curves, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and scanning vibrating electrode technique. The above electrochemical results show that FCDs is an effective corrosion inhibitor for copper in neutral solution, and its optimal corrosion inhibition efficiency exceeds to 88% at 70 ppm. The protective performance of FCDs depends on the coverage of adsorption film on copper surface. Its adsorption isotherm is in accordance with Langmuir adsorption model, involving both physisorption and chemisorption. Atomic force-distance curve directly quantifies the adhesion force between the adsorption film and copper surface.
The adsorption and anticorrosion mechanism of FCDs in neutral solution is proposed, which could provide theoretical guidance for design and synthesis of nanomaterial corrosion inhibitor.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper and its alloy are the key materials of plumbing tubes in the sea water systems [1, 2]. When copper plumbing tubes are exposed to saline water, the high concentration of Cl− in the seawater easily triggers pit corrosion on the copper surface. Corrosion inhibitors has been one of the most widespread methods to protect metal against corrosion, owing to the low cost, high efficiency, easy operation and so on. The corrosion inhibitors of copper [3–6] mainly can be divided into two categories: namely inorganic and organic corrosion inhibitors. Inorganic corrosion inhibitors can only provide relatively weak inhibition efficiency because of the instability of protective film. In comparison, organic corrosion inhibitors are much more effective corrosion inhibitors for copper and its alloy, especially in chloride-containing environment. However, most of organic corrosion inhibitors are toxic and hard to be biodegraded. In this case, it is necessary to develop new environmental-friendly corrosion inhibitors to replace traditional corrosion inhibitors.

Carbon dots (CDs) [7, 8] has gained wide attention as a type of zero-dimensional nanomaterial with excellent photoluminescence property, high biocompatibility, water solubility and low toxicity. In recent years, CDs have been gradually applied in the field of corrosion protection. Cui et al. [9, 10] prepared the N doped CDs with high water solubility and low cytotoxicity by using antibiotic aminosalicylic acid as precursor, and the CDs exhibited 93∼96% inhibition efficiency for Q235 carbon steel in HCl solution. Ye et al. [11] synthesized a 90% high-efficiency CDs inhibitor through pyrolysis of ammonium citrate for mild steel in hydrochloric acid environment. Cen et al. [12, 13] reported a effective N, S co-doped CDs inhibitor for carbon steel in CO2-saturated 3.5 wt% NaCl solution and for 5052 aluminum alloy in 0.1 mol/L HCl solution. However, most CDs inhibitors exhibited excellent corrosion inhibition for metal in acidic occasions, but possessed an unsatisfactory inhibition efficiency in neutral solutions. Few research is concerned about the preparation of high effective CDs inhibitor in neutral solution, and its adsorption and anticorrosion mechanism hasn't been made clear.

In this study, functionalized CDs (FCDs) inhibitor was firstly prepared using glucose as a carbon source for copper in neutral solution. The composition and structure of FCDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction meter (XRD), X-ray photoelectronic spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The inhibition efficiency of FCDs was evaluated by potentiodynamic polarization curves (PDP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and scanning vibrating electrode technique (SVET). Furthermore, the adsorption isotherm was further analyzed and corresponding anticorrosion mechanism was put forward.

The 4-amino-3-hydrazine-5-mercapto-1,2,4-triazole was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Glucose, ascorbic acid, sodium chloride and absolute ethyl alcohol were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Corporation.

The FCDs was prepared by a facile hydrothermal method. Firstly, 1 g glucose, 1 g ascorbic acid and 0.1 g 4-amino-3-hydrazine-5-mercapto-1,2,4-triazole were added into 40 mL deionized water and stirred until completely dissolved. Next, they were transferred to the Teflon hydrothermal reactor and then heated at 180 °C for 4 h. Then the resultant solution was dialyzed by a dialysis membrane with average molecular weight of 400 g/mol in deionized water for 24 h. Water was refreshed every 4 h during the dialysis process. Finally, the obtained product was dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 24 h. The schematic diagram of FCDs preparation is shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterizations of FCDs and corrosion products
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical component and chemical bonding of FCDs were analyzed by FT-IR, XRD, and XPS. Morphology and grain size of FCDs were characterized by TEM. The surface morphology of the copper samples immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with and without 50 ppm functionalized FCDs at 25 °C were examined by FE-SEM. The corresponding surface force curves on copper samples were measured by AFM.

Electrochemical corrosion behavior of copper was examined by a CS350 electrochemical workstation (CS310H, Corrtest, China) with traditional three-electrode system. The localized corrosion situation of copper was investigated by SVET technique (VersaSCAN, American). The microelectrode was at an average height of 200 μm above the test surface, vibrated with the amplitude of 30 μm at 1000 Hz, and the test area was 2000 μm × 2000 μm. Three parallel samples were tested for each experimental condition to assure the accuracy of consequences.

Fig. 2 presents the results of FTIR, XRD, XPS spectra and TEM images of FCDs inhibitor. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2(a)), the characteristic peaks at 3411 and 2929 cm−1 are related to the OH bond and CH bond in the alkyl chain. In the case of 4-amino-3-hydrazino-1,2,4-triazol-5-thiol, the adsorption peaks at 1420 and 1385 cm−1 are attributed to CN and C = S groups respectively [14, 15]. Moreover, the formation of N C = O bond at 1578 cm−1 [12, 16] suggests that 4-amino-3-hydrazino-1,2,4-triazol-5-thiol was successfully anchored on glucose-based CDs by the amidation reaction. In the XRD spectra (Fig. 2(b)), a series of characteristic diffraction peaks appear at 31.71°, 45.49°, 66.36° and 75.11° This can be attributed to the formation of carbon structure [17, 18] with fixed crystal pattern according to the standard PDF card 00–046–0943. Fig. 2(c) shows the XPS full spectrum of FCDs, and it mainly contain C, N, S and O elements. Corresponding, the XPS high-resolution spectra of C, S and N are presented in Fig. 2(e)∼(f). For the C 1 s spectrum, it could be decomposed to three peaks. They include N C = O, CO and CC groups at 288.2, 286.3 and 284.2 eV respectively. Furthermore, the formation of N C = O bond suggests that the amidogen of 4-Amino-3-hydrazino-1,2,4-triazol-5-thiol successfully reacts with the carboxyl of glucose-based CDs. Meanwhile, the S 2p spectrum could be fitted with two peaks, C = S bond at 163.8 eV and -C-S(O)x-C bond at 168.4 eV [12, 19-21], which illustrates that the synthesized FCDs are S-doped CDs. In addition, the N 1 s spectrum can be divided into N = C and NC bonds, which center on 400.9 eV and 399.7 eV respectively [22, 23]. These results are in accordance with the consequence of FTIR spectrum. Fig. 2(g)∼(h) show TEM images of FCDs inhibitor, which present spherical structure with size of 5–7 nm.

Fig. 3 shows EIS diagrams and corresponding equivalent circuits of Cu in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with different concentrations of FCDs inhibitor. For copper in 3.5 wt% NaCl (Fig. 3(a)), the Nyquist diagram exhibits three time constants, corresponding to the surface oxide film at high frequency, charge transfer process at medium frequency and diffusion process at low frequency respectively. It can be fitted by the equivalent circuit in Fig. 3(f). Among them, Rs, Rct, Rf, Wd, CPEdl and CPEf , represent solution resistance, charge transfer resistance, film resistance, Warburg diffusion impedance of metal oxide film on bare copper electrode, constant phase angle element of double electric layer and surface film, respectively. With the prolong of immersion time, corrosion process is slightly retarded. After adding FCDs inhibitor, the diameter of capacitive reactance arc sharply increases. When adding low concentration (30 ppm) of FCDs inhibitor, it shows similar impedance characteristic and equivalent circuit with that in the absence of FCDs. Their differences are that the former film is copper oxide film, while the latter film is the protection film. When addition concentration of FCDs inhibitor is more than 30 ppm, the impedance spectra shows two capacitive arcs, and the time constant disappears at low frequency related to the diffusion process (Fig. 3(g)). It suggests that charge transfer process is rate-determining step rather than diffusion process. As the FCDs concentration increases, impedance modulus firstly shows an ascend tendency and then a descend tendency, and it reaches the maximum values at 70 ppm. The resistance values firstly increase then decrease with the extend of immersion time, and all the concentration of FCDs exhibit the optimum protective effects at 6 h immersion. In order to further evaluate the inhibition performances of FCDs, the inhibition efficiency η is obtained according to the following equation [24]:
(1)
η =
R
c t
−
R
c t , 0
R
c t
× 100 %

Where the Rct and Rct,
0 represent the charge transfer resistance with and without corrosion inhibitors respectively. The corresponding fitting results are summarized in Table 1
(supporting materials). When addition concentration of FCDs inhibitor is 30 ppm, the maximum η value only reaches 83.7% lower than that at high concentration. It may be attributed to that the surface coverage of FCDs at the copper electrode is fairly low and thus its protective effect is unsatisfactory. With rising FCDs concentration, it exhibits the best exhibition efficiency at 70 ppm, ∼88%. However, the corrosion inhibition efficiency for high concentration FCDs at 100 ppm decreases in the late stage of immersion. Owing to that the interaction force of FCDs nanoparticle is stronger than that of FCDs on copper surface at higher concentration 100 ppm, the phenomenon of FCDs aggregation occurs (seeing supporting material Fig. S1). The aggregation of FCDs nanomaterial could result in the decrease of adsorption amount of corrosion inhibitor on copper surface, thus weakening its corrosion inhibition efficiency.

SVET can be applied as a straightforward tool to visualize the localized corrosion of copper in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution in the absence and presence of FCDs. The potential difference distribution diagrams for Cu after different immersion time in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution without and with FCDs inhibitor are presented in Fig. 4
. The high positive potential difference appears on copper surface in pure 3.5 wt% NaCl solution and corresponding current density is detected, declaring that copper is severely corroded. After the addition of FCDs inhibitor, the measured potential difference and current on copper surface significantly decreases, and the detected current density is extremely low. It suggests that FCDs inhibitor can retard the corrosion behavior of copper in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. With the extend of immersion time, potential difference gradually increases for copper in pure NaCl solution, while it decreases after the introduction of FCDs. It confirms that the FCDs inhibitor could adsorb on the copper surface and hold back further corrosion of copper.

Fig. 5 shows the polarization curves of Cu after 24 h immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with different concentrations of FCDs inhibitor. The corresponding Tafel fitting parameters are presented in Table 2 (supporting materials), such as corrosion potential (Ecorr ), corrosion current density (icorr ), anodic Tafel slope (ba ), cathodic Tafel slope (bc ), and corrosion inhibition efficiency (IE). Thereinto, the IE values are acquired through the following equation [25, 26]:
(2)
IE =
I
corr , 0
−
I
c o r r
I
corr , 0
× 100 %

Where Icorr,
0 and Icorr denote the corrosion current density in the absence and presence of FCDs inhibitor respectively. After the addition of FCDs inhibitor, the corrosion potential shifts toward the positive direction and corrosion current density decreases, owing to hindering the copper anodic dissolution. When the concentration of FCDs inhibitor is 70 ppm, the corrosion current density decreases markedly from 2.54 μA⋅cm−2 without FCDs inhibitor to 0.428 μA⋅cm−2, and it exhibits the highest corrosion inhibition efficiency, 83.12%. The above results agree well with the consequences of impedance fitting data.

Moreover, the copper samples after 24 h immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution in the absence and presence of FCDs inhibitor were analyzed by atomic force curves and surface morphology. The force-distance curves measured on copper electrodes with and without FCDs are shown in Fig. 6 (a). The five points on each sample are selected to measure the surface adhesive force, and the result of statistic analysis is presented in Fig. 6(b). There is nearly no adhesive force characteristic recorded on copper surface without FCDs inhibitor in the force-distance curves, and the measured average adhesive force is about 0 nN. After the introduction of FCDs inhibitor, obvious adhesive force is measured and its average value reaches 2.5 nN. This confirms that the copper surface is covered by the FCDs inhibitor film [27, 28]. Fig. 6(c)∼(d) reveal the surface corrosion morphology of copper after 24 h immersion without and with FCDs inhibitor. Obviously, there are a large amount of corrosion products on copper surface without FCDs inhibitor. It is attributed to the successive attack of corrosive chloride ions. However, after adding FCDs inhibitor, the amount of corrosion products is greatly deduced and some scratches are observed on the copper surface, indicating that the corrosion process is significantly inhibited. The above results of force-distance curves and surface morphology on copper surface confirm the formation of FCDs film and its excellent inhibition effect for copper, which are consistent with the consequences of electrochemical tests.

The adsorption isotherm was used to further research the inhibition mechanism of FCDs inhibitor. The results of corrosion inhibition efficiency from EIS data are shown in Fig. 7 (a). It is seen that FCDs inhibitor obtains the maximum corrosion inhibition efficiency at 4∼8 h. Supposing that FCDs inhibitor reaches a quasi-adsorption equilibrium at 4 h, 6 h and 8 h immersion, it is found that the experimental data fit well with the Langmuir adsorption isotherms. The corresponding adsorption isotherms are acquired by the following equation [29]:
(3)
θ
1 − θ
=
K ads
C

Where θ, Kads and C are the surface coverage, adsorption equilibrium constant and inhibitor concentration respectively. Among them, θ is approximately regarded as inhibition efficiency. The functions of C/θ versus C at 4 h, 6 h and 8 h immersion are displayed in Fig. 7(b)∼(d). The linear regression coefficients of R2 are 0.9992, 0.9998 and 0.9998 respectively. They suggest the perfect linear relationship and conform to the Langmuir adsorption model. Moreover, the calculated values of Kads and the adsorption free energy (∆Gads ) are presented in the diagrams. The ∆Gads is deduced by the following equation [10]:
(4)
Δ
G ads
= − RTIn
( 1000
K
a d s
)

Where R and T are the molar gas constant and absolute temperature (298 K) respectively. The values of Kads at 4 h, 6 h and 8 h immersion are 0.29545, 0.33684 and 0.26288 L/mg, respectively. The highest Kads value at 6 h indicates the strongest adsorption ability of FCDs inhibitor on the copper/solution interface. Besides, the ∆Gads values could be used as a parameter to determine the adsorption type. Generally, when the value of ∆Gads is lower than −40 KJ/mol, it belongs to the chemical adsorption; when the value of ∆Gads is higher than −20 KJ/mol, it belongs to the physical adsorption; when the value of ∆Gads is between −20 KJ/mol and −40 KJ/mol, it belongs to physicochemical adsorption [30, 31]. From ∆Gads values in the Fig. 7(b)∼(d), the adsorption types of FCDs inhibitor at different immersion time all involve physicochemical adsorption. Among them, the ∆Gads value (−31.55 KJ/mol) at 6 h immersion is the lowest, suggesting that the adsorption of FCDs inhibitor is easier to proceed. The highest Kads value and the lowest ∆Gads value account for the best inhibition efficiency of FCDs inhibitor at 6 h immersion.

In the present study, the prepared glucose-based FCDs as a corrosion inhibitor for copper is firstly applied in neutral 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. To further understand the interaction between copper and FCDs inhibitor, the adsorption and anti-corrosion mechanism is put forward shown in Fig. 8 . The cathodic oxygen reduction reaction of copper in neutral NaCl solution is as follows:
(5)
O 2
+ 2
H 2
O + 4
e
−
→ 4 O
H −

While the copper anodic reaction could be given in the following steps [28]:
(6)
2 Cu +
1 2
O 2
→ C
u 2
O
(7)
Cu + C
l
−
→ C u C
l
a d s
+
e −
(8)
CuC
l
a d s
+ C
l −
→ C u C
l 2 −

Meanwhile, the hydrolysis of
C u C
l 2 −
is expressed as follows:
(9)
C u C
l 2 −
+
1 2
H 2
O →
1 2
C
u 2
O + 2 C
l
−
+
H +
Among them, the reaction (8) is the rate-determining step, mainly influenced by the diffusion process of the transportation of soluble copper species or dissolved oxygen.

According to the ∆Gads value of FCDs inhibitor, the interaction of FCDs with copper in neutral solution belongs to a physicochemical mixed adsorption. Firstly, FCDs inhibitor adsorbs on the copper surface through electrostatic interaction (physisorption) [30]. Secondly, the hydroxyl, carboxyl and heterocyclic groups in the FCDs could be used as active sites for the coordination with copper or copper ion/complex (chemical adsorption). Its coordination is attributed to the donor-acceptor interaction [32] between lone pair electrons and vacant 3d orbitals of copper or copper ion/complex, which can be demonstrated by FTIR, XPS (Fig. 2), SEM and AFM (Fig. 6).

The protection mechanism of FCDs could be briefly described as follows. On one hand, the adsorption film of FCDs inhibitor is an effective physical barrier to hinder the invasion of corrosive chloride ions, oxygen and water molecules into the copper surface [33]; on the other hand, the network-structure chelate between FCDs and copper or copper ion/complex forms during the diffusion of dissolved copper into the NaCl solution [34]. It not only isolates from the direct contact of corrosive medium on copper, but also impedes the electron transportation and thus inhibits the electrochemical reactions between anode and cathode.

In this study, the preparation, structure and corrosion behaviours of FCDs inhibitor for copper are researched in neutral 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The detailed conclusions can be summarized as follows:
(1) The FTIR, XRD and XPS spectra indicate 4-amino-3-hydrazine-5-mercapto-1,2,4-triazole successfully anchors on glucose-based carbon dots by amidation reaction. TEM images show that FCDs grain size is about 5–7 nm.
(2) The EIS, PDP and SVET results confirm that FCDs inhibitor exhibits the optimal inhibition efficiency (∼88%) at 70 ppm for copper in neutral NaCl solution. With rising FCDs concentration, the inhibition efficiency presents a tendency of increasing firstly and then decreasing.
(3) The protective layer of FCDs inhibitor obeys the Langmuir isotherm adsorption, and its inhibition efficiency is related to the surface coverage of adsorption film on copper surface. According to the ∆Gads data from Langmuir adsorption isotherm, the interaction of FCDs with copper involves both chemisorption and physisorption. AFM force-distance curve directly quantifies the adhesion force between the adsorption film and copper surface.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors want to thank the following foundations for the financial supports to this work: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant No. 51971067, No. 52001080); The China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (grant 2020M682650).
